content_html:
Launch HN: Screenpipe (YC S26) – Record how you work and turn that into agents
79 points by louis030195 21 hours ago | hide | past | favorite | 60 comments

Hi Hacker News, I'm Louis. I built Screenpipe (https://screenpipe.com), an app that records your screen and audio locally (only!), and gives AI agents a searchable memory of what you've seen, said, and heard. This makes it easier to automate your repetitive tasks, turn them into SOPs (Standard Operating Procedure) and so on.

I made a HN-style demo video at https://www.tella.tv/video/build-your-ai-second-brain-with-s... and there’s a marketing video at https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=c1jV6E9pyug.

I’ve been obsessed with this for a long time. I’ve been maintaining a “second brain” since 2020, in which I would store journals, handwritten notes, music I listen to, projects I'm working on, conversations I have with people, personal CRM etc. I experimented a lot of RAG in the early days with ParlAI, hundreds of fine-tuned GPT2 models, and GPT3 (https://forum.obsidian.md/t/fine-tuning-openai-api-gpt3-on-y...). Later I built Ava, the first Obsidian AI plugin, which grew to a few thousands of users quickly. It then became Embedbase, an API to make it easier to build AI apps powered by RAG.

What I learned from all this is how important it is for the models to have context about what you’re doing on your computer, in order to get them to do what you want.

In the early days there was fine tuning but it was too much pain, then there was tool calling so that AI can access software you use but still kinda not autonomous enough. needing micro management. Then MCP came, but it felt too static, and non technical users struggled to build and use MCP. Then we got skills. Most recently we’ve seen Karpathy’s LLM-maintained wiki, Garry's GBrain, etc., where an agent incrementally maintains a persistent collection of Markdown pages. New sources update entity pages, strengthen or contradict existing claims, and improve a synthesis that compounds over time. I like this pattern, but it still begins with someone selecting and importing the sources. There is still no way AI can know what you and your company are doing every day, across apps, not just inside of apps.

Of course, not everyone wants this. But I do! I want AI to know what I'm doing and never lose memory ever again, and I want it to use the same software that humans do, without painful context switches.

I started building Screenpipe for myself in 2024 - a CLI to record your screen and plug this context into AI. An HN user posted it in 2024 (https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=41695840) and that discussion influenced the product. The most useful criticism concerned recording consent, local security, CPU usage, signal-to-noise, and whether agents could act on top of the data.

The naive implementation started from continuously recording video and running OCR over every frame. But that creates duplicate data, consumes substantial resources (it basically turns your computer into a space heater!), and discards structure the operating system already knows. Screenpipe now instead listens for events such as app switches, clicks, typing pauses, scrolling, and idle fallbacks. When something meaningful changes, it pairs a screenshot with the operating system’s accessibility tree at the same timestamp. OCR is used when structured accessibility data is unavailable. We also capture audio continuously, identify speakers and transcribe locally through Parakeet/Whisper or using cloud models.

Everything is indexed in a local SQLite database, mp4 files, and sometimes md files. An AI friendly API on port 3030 is open for agents, with authentication and a MCP and skills.

Once Screenpipe has been up and running for a while, you can use it through our built-in chat, Claude, ChatGPT, Hermes, Openclaw, or any agent, to do things like:

- adding context to your current chat, e.g. "gather all context about task X", then requiring less prompts to achieve your goal

- retrieve information, e.g. "retrieve the tasks i was working on from 8 am to 4 pm, make a list of what got done and what's left"

- create and maintain a personal wiki / second brain for your agents: "every 1h organize everything i do in projects, people, tasks, meetings in my Obsidian vault as markdown files and folders"

- create automations: whenever i visit someone's profile on linkedin, update my crm

- find automation opportunities: look at everything my team has done this week and turn it into a list of automation opportunities

Screenpipe data is stored locally, though we also offer an enterprise plan to discover automation opportunities and for that the company decides where the data lives. We built our own AI PII model to redact sensitive information, it runs locally on Apple MLX or Windows DirectML, we also support cloud confidential inference for low end devices, although our local models are meant to use <1% CPU and <400 mb RAM. Users can set apps, windows, and urls to filter, in addition to browser incognito mode.

We also support recording schedules and other privacy features.

Most of our codebase is written in Rust, MLX, Onnx, we like cidre or direct C call for Apple APIs and windows-rs for Windows API. We also experimentally support Linux.

We have a desktop app (https://screenpipe.com/how-to-install) and a CLI:

  npx screenpipe record

You can run that without creating an account. All the code is source-available at https://github.com/screenpipe/screenpipe. We took the dreaded step of making our own Screenpipe Commercial License. I know HN strongly prefers OSI open source (MIT/Apache/etc.) but couldn’t find a sustainable way to keep developing Screenpipe while companies were using it commercially for free. So now personal non-commercial, nonprofit, educational, and research use is free, but commercial use requires a license.

Versions released before the license change remain available under MIT. We have a free tier, and other plans, including Enterprise which helps companies find automation opportunities.

Would love to hear any feedback, things you've done with screenpipe, or features you'd want

blocks.html_en:
Hi Hacker News, I'm Louis. I built Screenpipe (https://screenpipe.com), an app that records your screen and audio locally (only!), and gives AI agents a searchable memory of what you've seen, said, and heard. This makes it easier to automate your repetitive tasks, turn them into SOPs (Standard Operating Procedure) and so on.

I made a HN-style demo video at https://www.tella.tv/video/build-your-ai-second-brain-with-s... and there’s a marketing video at https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=c1jV6E9pyug.

I’ve been obsessed with this for a long time. I’ve been maintaining a “second brain” since 2020, in which I would store journals, handwritten notes, music I listen to, projects I'm working on, conversations I have with people, personal CRM etc. I experimented a lot of RAG in the early days with ParlAI, hundreds of fine-tuned GPT2 models, and GPT3 (https://forum.obsidian.md/t/fine-tuning-openai-api-gpt3-on-y...). Later I built Ava, the first Obsidian AI plugin, which grew to a few thousands of users quickly. It then became Embedbase, an API to make it easier to build AI apps powered by RAG.

What I learned from all this is how important it is for the models to have context about what you’re doing on your computer, in order to get them to do what you want.

In the early days there was fine tuning but it was too much pain, then there was tool calling so that AI can access software you use but still kinda not autonomous enough. needing micro management. Then MCP came, but it felt too static, and non technical users struggled to build and use MCP. Then we got skills. Most recently we’ve seen Karpathy’s LLM-maintained wiki, Garry's GBrain, etc., where an agent incrementally maintains a persistent collection of Markdown pages. New sources update entity pages, strengthen or contradict existing claims, and improve a synthesis that compounds over time. I like this pattern, but it still begins with someone selecting and importing the sources. There is still no way AI can know what you and your company are doing every day, across apps, not just inside of apps.

Of course, not everyone wants this. But I do! I want AI to know what I'm doing and never lose memory ever again, and I want it to use the same software that humans do, without painful context switches.

I started building Screenpipe for myself in 2024 - a CLI to record your screen and plug this context into AI. An HN user posted it in 2024 (https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=41695840) and that discussion influenced the product. The most useful criticism concerned recording consent, local security, CPU usage, signal-to-noise, and whether agents could act on top of the data.

The naive implementation started from continuously recording video and running OCR over every frame. But that creates duplicate data, consumes substantial resources (it basically turns your computer into a space heater!), and discards structure the operating system already knows. Screenpipe now instead listens for events such as app switches, clicks, typing pauses, scrolling, and idle fallbacks. When something meaningful changes, it pairs a screenshot with the operating system’s accessibility tree at the same timestamp. OCR is used when structured accessibility data is unavailable. We also capture audio continuously, identify speakers and transcribe locally through Parakeet/Whisper or using cloud models.

Everything is indexed in a local SQLite database, mp4 files, and sometimes md files. An AI friendly API on port 3030 is open for agents, with authentication and a MCP and skills.

Once Screenpipe has been up and running for a while, you can use it through our built-in chat, Claude, ChatGPT, Hermes, Openclaw, or any agent, to do things like:

- adding context to your current chat, e.g. "gather all context about task X", then requiring less prompts to achieve your goal

- retrieve information, e.g. "retrieve the tasks i was working on from 8 am to 4 pm, make a list of what got done and what's left"

- create and maintain a personal wiki / second brain for your agents: "every 1h organize everything i do in projects, people, tasks, meetings in my Obsidian vault as markdown files and folders"

- create automations: whenever i visit someone's profile on linkedin, update my crm

- find automation opportunities: look at everything my team has done this week and turn it into a list of automation opportunities

Screenpipe data is stored locally, though we also offer an enterprise plan to discover automation opportunities and for that the company decides where the data lives. We built our own AI PII model to redact sensitive information, it runs locally on Apple MLX or Windows DirectML, we also support cloud confidential inference for low end devices, although our local models are meant to use <1% CPU and <400 mb RAM. Users can set apps, windows, and urls to filter, in addition to browser incognito mode.

We also support recording schedules and other privacy features.

Most of our codebase is written in Rust, MLX, Onnx, we like cidre or direct C call for Apple APIs and windows-rs for Windows API. We also experimentally support Linux.

We have a desktop app (https://screenpipe.com/how-to-install) and a CLI:

  npx screenpipe record

You can run that without creating an account. All the code is source-available at https://github.com/screenpipe/screenpipe. We took the dreaded step of making our own Screenpipe Commercial License. I know HN strongly prefers OSI open source (MIT/Apache/etc.) but couldn’t find a sustainable way to keep developing Screenpipe while companies were using it commercially for free. So now personal non-commercial, nonprofit, educational, and research use is free, but commercial use requires a license.

Versions released before the license change remain available under MIT. We have a free tier, and other plans, including Enterprise which helps companies find automation opportunities.

Would love to hear any feedback, things you've done with screenpipe, or features you'd want

blocks.html_en: In the early days there was fine tuning but it was too much pain, then there was tool calling so that AI can access software you use but still kinda not autonomous enough. needing micro management. Then MCP came, but it felt too static, and non technical users struggled to build and use MCP. Then we got skills. Most recently we’ve seen Karpathy’s LLM-maintained wiki, Garry's GBrain, etc., where an agent incrementally maintains a persistent collection of Markdown pages. New sources update entity pages, strengthen or contradict existing claims, and improve a synthesis that compounds over time. I like this pattern, but it still begins with someone selecting and importing the sources. There is still no way AI can know what you and your company are doing every day, across apps, not just inside of apps.

sentences.en: Most recently we’ve seen Karpathy’s LLM-maintained wiki, Garry's GBrain, etc., where an agent incrementally maintains a persistent collection of Markdown pages.

blocks.html_en: Of course, not everyone wants this. But I do! I want AI to know what I'm doing and never lose memory ever again, and I want it to use the same software that humans do, without painful context switches.

blocks.html_en: I started building Screenpipe for myself in 2024 - a CLI to record your screen and plug this context into AI. An HN user posted it in 2024 (https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=41695840) and that discussion influenced the product. The most useful criticism concerned recording consent, local security, CPU usage, signal-to-noise, and whether agents could act on top of the data.

sentences.zh: 我在 2024 年开始为自己构建 Screenpipe——一个用于记录屏幕并将此上下文插入 AI 的 CLI。

sentences.en: An HN user posted it in 2024 (https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=41695840) and that discussion influenced the product.

sentences.en: The most useful criticism concerned recording consent, local security, CPU usage, signal-to-noise, and whether agents could act on top of the data.
sentences.zh: 最有用的批评涉及记录同意、本地安全、CPU 使用率、信噪比以及代理是否可以根据数据采取行动。

blocks.html_en: The naive implementation started from continuously recording video and running OCR over every frame. But that creates duplicate data, consumes substantial resources (it basically turns your computer into a space heater!), and discards structure the operating system already knows. Screenpipe now instead listens for events such as app switches, clicks, typing pauses, scrolling, and idle fallbacks. When something meaningful changes, it pairs a screenshot with the operating system’s accessibility tree at the same timestamp. OCR is used when structured accessibility data is unavailable. We also capture audio continuously, identify speakers and transcribe locally through Parakeet/Whisper or using cloud models.

sentences.en: The naive implementation started from continuously recording video and running OCR over every frame.
sentences.zh: 简单的实现是从连续录制视频并在每一帧上运行 OCR 开始的。

sentences.en: But that creates duplicate data, consumes substantial resources (it basically turns your computer into a space heater!), and discards structure the operating system already knows.

sentences.en: Screenpipe now instead listens for events such as app switches, clicks, typing pauses, scrolling, and idle fallbacks.
sentences.zh: Screenpipe 现在改为侦听应用程序切换、单击、打字暂停、滚动和空闲回退等事件。

sentences.zh: 当发生有意义的变化时，它会将屏幕截图与同一时间戳的操作系统的可访问性树配对。

sentences.en: OCR is used when structured accessibility data is unavailable.
sentences.zh: 当结构化可访问性数据不可用时，使用 OCR。

sentences.en: We also capture audio continuously, identify speakers and transcribe locally through Parakeet/Whisper or using cloud models.
sentences.zh: 我们还连续捕获音频、识别说话者并通过 Parakeet/Whisper 或使用云模型进行本地转录。

blocks.html_en: Everything is indexed in a local SQLite database, mp4 files, and sometimes md files. An AI friendly API on port 3030 is open for agents, with authentication and a MCP and skills.

sentences.zh: 端口 3030 上的 AI 友好 API 向代理开放，具有身份验证、MCP 和技能。

blocks.html_en: Once Screenpipe has been up and running for a while, you can use it through our built-in chat, Claude, ChatGPT, Hermes, Openclaw, or any agent, to do things like:

blocks.html_en: - retrieve information, e.g. "retrieve the tasks i was working on from 8 am to 4 pm, make a list of what got done and what's left"

blocks.html_en: Screenpipe data is stored locally, though we also offer an enterprise plan to discover automation opportunities and for that the company decides where the data lives. We built our own AI PII model to redact sensitive information, it runs locally on Apple MLX or Windows DirectML, we also support cloud confidential inference for low end devices, although our local models are meant to use <1% CPU and <400 mb RAM. Users can set apps, windows, and urls to filter, in addition to browser incognito mode.

So now personal non-commercial, nonprofit, educational, and research use is free, but commercial use requires a license.

content_html_zh: 启动 HN: Screenpipe (YC S26) – 记录你的工作方式并将其转化为代理79 分 by louis030195 21 小时前 |隐藏 |过去|最喜欢 | 60 条评论嗨，黑客新闻，我是路易斯。 我构建了 Screenpipe (https://screenpipe.com)，这是一款可以在本地记录您的屏幕和音频（仅！）的应用程序，并为 AI 代理提供您所见、所说和所听内容的可搜索记忆。 这使得你可以更容易地自动化你的重复性任务，将它们变成SOP（标准操作程序）等等。我在https://www.tella.tv/video/build-your-ai-second-brain-with-s...制作了一个HN风格的演示视频...并且在https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=c1jV6E9pyug上有一个营销视频。我已经对此着迷了很长时间。 自 2020 年以来，我一直在维护“第二大脑”，其中我会存储日记、手写笔记、我听的音乐、我正在从事的项目、我与人的对话、个人 CRM 等。早期，我使用 ParlAI、数百个经过微调的 GPT2 模型和 GPT3 尝试了很多 RAG （https://forum.obsidian.md/t/fine-tuning-openai-api-gpt3-on-y...）。后来我构建了 Ava，第一个 Obsidian AI 插件，很快就拥有了数千名用户。 然后它变成了 Embedbase，一个 API，可以更轻松地构建由 RAG 驱动的 AI 应用程序。我从这一切中学到的是，对于模型来说，了解你在计算机上所做的事情是多么重要，以便让它们做你想做的事情。早期有微调，但太痛苦了，然后有工具调用，以便 AI 可以访问你使用的软件，但仍然不够自治。 需要微观管理。 然后 MCP 出现了，但感觉太静态了，非技术用户很难构建和使用 MCP。 然后我们就有了技能。 最近我们看到了 Karpathy 的 LLM 维护的 wiki、Garry 的 GBrain 等，其中代理增量地维护 Markdown 页面的持久集合。 新来源更新实体页面，加强或反驳现有主张，并改进随着时间的推移而复合的综合。 我喜欢这种模式，但它仍然从选择和导入源开始。 人工智能仍然无法知道你和你的公司每天在应用程序中做什么，而不仅仅是应用程序内部。当然，并不是每个人都希望这样。 但我愿意！ 我希望 AI 知道我在做什么，并且永远不会再失去记忆，我希望它使用与人类相同的软件，而无需痛苦的上下文切换。我在 2024 年开始为自己构建 Screenpipe - 一个 CLI，用于记录屏幕并将此上下文插入 AI。 一位 HN 用户于 2024 年发布了该文章 (https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=41695840)，该讨论影响了该产品。 最有用的批评涉及记录同意、本地安全、CPU 使用率、信噪比以及代理是否可以根据数据采取行动。简单的实施是从连续记录视频并在每一帧上运行 OCR 开始的。 但这会创建重复的数据，消耗大量资源（它基本上将您的计算机变成了空间加热器！），并丢弃了操作系统已经知道的结构。 Screenpipe 现在改为侦听应用程序切换、单击、打字暂停、滚动和空闲回退等事件。 当发生有意义的变化时，它会将屏幕截图与同一时间戳的操作系统的可访问性树配对。 当结构化可访问性数据不可用时，使用 OCR。 我们还连续捕获音频、识别说话者并通过 Parakeet/Whisper 或使用云模型进行本地转录。所有内容都在本地 SQLite 数据库、mp4 文件（有时还包括 md 文件）中建立索引。 端口 3030 上的 AI 友好 API 向代理开放，具有身份验证、MCP 和技能。一旦 Screenpipe 启动并运行一段时间，您可以通过我们的内置聊天、Claude、ChatGPT、Hermes、Openclaw 或任何代理使用它，执行以下操作：- 向当前聊天添加上下文，例如“收集有关任务 X 的所有上下文”，然后需要更少的提示来实现您的目标 - 检索信息，例如“检索我从早上 8 点到下午 4 点正在处理的任务，列出已完成的内容和剩余的内容”- 为您的代理创建和维护个人 wiki/第二大脑：“每 1 小时将我在黑曜石保险库中的项目、人员、任务、会议中所做的一切整理为 Markdown 文件和文件夹”- 创建自动化：每当我在 linkedin 上访问某人的个人资料时，更新我的客户关系管理 - 寻找自动化机会：查看我的团队本周所做的所有事情，并将其变成一个自动化机会列表 Screenpipe 数据存储在本地，但我们还提供企业计划来发现自动化机会，并由公司决定数据所在的位置。 我们构建了自己的 AI PII 模型来编辑敏感信息，它在 Apple MLX 或 Windows DirectML 上本地运行，我们还支持低端设备的云机密推理，尽管我们的本地模型旨在使用 <1% CPU 和 <400 mb RAM。 除了浏览器隐身模式外，用户还可以设置要过滤的应用程序、窗口和 url。我们还支持录制时间表和其他隐私功能。我们的大部分代码库都是用 Rust、MLX、Onnx 编写的，我们喜欢针对 Apple API 的 cidre 或直接 C 调用，针对 Windows API 的 windows-rs。 我们还实验性地支持 Linux。我们有一个桌面应用程序 (https://screenpipe.com/how-to-install) 和一个 CLI：npx screenpipe record 您可以在不创建帐户的情况下运行它。 所有代码均可在 https://github.com/screenpipe/screenpipe 上获取源代码。我们迈出了可怕的一步，制作了我们自己的 Screenpipe 商业许可证。 我知道 HN 非常喜欢 OSI 开源（MIT/Apache/等），但在公司免费商业使用 Screenpipe 时找不到可持续的方法来继续开发 Screenpipe。 因此，现在个人非商业、非营利、教育和研究用途是免费的，但商业用途需要许可证。许可证更改之前发布的版本在 MIT 下仍然可用。 我们有免费套餐和其他计划，包括帮助公司寻找自动化机会的企业版。希望听到任何反馈、您使用 screenpipe 所做的事情或您需要帮助的功能
